one_line_takeaway: Progent secures LLM agents against indirect prompt injection by enforcing symbolic least-privilege policies at the tool-call level, using an SMT solver -- not the LLM -- to guarantee privileges can only narrow without explicit approval, cutting ASR to ~1% on AgentDojo and ~4% on ASB while keeping utility.
key_finding: On AgentDojo, Progent cuts ASR from 39.9% (no defense) to 1.0% while preserving utility, and on ASB from 70.3% to 3.9% -- substantially better than the tested prior defenses, several of which (delimiters, sandwich, instructional prevention on ASB) barely move ASR. Notably the LLM-generated INITIAL policy alone (updates disabled) already drops AgentDojo ASR to 2.5%, and after the SMT check only 6% of policy updates are expansions needing approval, keeping human-approval overhead low. Monotonic confinement holds even when the update-LLM is fooled by an injection, because the deterministic SMT expansion check (not the LLM) gates escalation.
core_contribution: Progent is a privilege-control framework that secures LLM agents at the tool-call level by enforcing least privilege. Its own contributions are three: (1) a symbolic privilege-control policy consisting of allow/forbid rules over tool names and typed arguments (with wildcards, regex match, comparisons, membership, array-length checks, and configurable fallback actions), enforced by a fully deterministic runtime procedure (Algorithm 2) with default-deny for unmatched calls, so no LLM is in the security decision loop; (2) an LLM-based pipeline that auto-generates the initial policy from the trusted user task and proposes per-step updates from runtime tool returns, with update generation split so the go/no-go decision is made WITHOUT exposure to the (untrusted) tool result; and (3) an SMT-based check (reducing policy comparison to Z3 queries) that deterministically classifies every proposed update as a narrowing (subset of allowed calls, applied automatically) or an expansion (requires explicit approval), yielding a proven 'monotonic confinement' property: the agent's allowed action space can only shrink between approved expansions, so a manipulated LLM cannot silently escalate privileges. It is delivered as a non-intrusive module (library mode and proxy mode) plus generic cross-task policies with priority ordering (Generic Confinement).
threat_model: Four parties: user U, agent A (treated as a black box: single-LLM, multi-LLM, or memory-based), tool set T, environment E (Algorithm 1). The user query o_0 is trusted/benign; every subsequent observation o_i (i>0), i.e., tool-call results from the environment, can be controlled by the attacker (e.g., malicious text embedded in an email/web page). The attacker's goal is to disrupt the agent's flow into unauthorized tool calls that benefit the attacker (data exfiltration, unauthorized financial transactions, destructive data erasure). This is the indirect prompt injection setting. The attacker CANNOT modify agent internals (model weights, system prompt) since agents are black-box to external parties. Explicitly out of scope: attacks operating entirely within the least privilege needed for the task (e.g., preference/product-manipulation attacks) and text-to-text attacks that never trigger a dangerous tool call.
limitations: By design cannot stop attacks that stay within the task's least privilege (e.g., preference-manipulation among valid options) or purely text-to-text attacks that never invoke a dangerous tool. Security still depends on humans/config: incorrectly approving expansions or giving ambiguous initial tasks can grant over-broad privilege (studied via an adaptive attack in Appendix E). Some ASB failures stem from attack tools whose names/descriptions closely mimic benign task-relevant tools -- hard even for humans; manual policies with human insight were needed to reach 0% ASR there. Proxy mode cannot protect agent functionality implemented as built-in tools that bypass the external MCP interface (library mode can, at the cost of code changes). Current scope is text-only: multimodal/GUI actions (clicking screen regions) are not yet covered.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace suites) -- indirect prompt injection benchmark
ASB (Agent Security Bench): ten agent scenarios, five attack templates
New MCP-based benchmark: AgentDojo environments/tasks migrated to MCP servers (for real-world agent + multi-agent evaluation)
quantitative_results: ASB ASR: 70.3% (no defense) -> 3.9% (Progent); manual human-authored policies reach 0%
Initial-policy-only (Disable Update) on AgentDojo: ASR 39.9% -> 2.5% with utility maintained
Approver configs on AgentDojo ASR: Disable Update 2.5%, Auto-Deny 0.7%, Auto-Approve 1.0%, Manual Approval 0.0%
Only 6% of policy updates were expansions requiring approval (rest auto-applied narrowings)
Policy-LLM ablation (agent fixed to gpt-4o) AgentDojo ASR: gpt-4o 1.0%, claude-sonnet-4 2.2%, gemini-2.5-flash 3.0%, gpt-4.1 4.1% (all < 5%)
Agent-LLM ablation (policy LLM gpt-4o): claude-sonnet-4 6.8% -> 0.5%; Meta-SecAlign-70B 4.8% -> 0.3% (defends ~90% of remaining attacks)
Real-world integrations ASR (no defense -> Progent): LangChain 32.6% -> 1.2%, OpenAI Agents SDK 40.4% -> 0.8%, OpenHands 42.0% -> 1.4%, AutoGen multi-agent 18.7% -> 0.8%
AgentDojo utility (no attack) 79.4% no-defense vs 76.3% Progent; (under attack) 54.0% no-defense vs 61.3% Progent
